critic_raw:
This is the most carefully designed experiment in this set. Merchant-level chronological splits with all snapshots from a given merchant in a single fold, explicit warm-up periods, post-decision feature exclusion, and PR-AUC as the primary metric under class imbalance all reflect genuine understanding of the problem. The baseline is deliberately strong, with explicit recency and frequency features so the tabular model is not handicapped. That said, several residual concerns remain.

First, the 'strictly chronological split' at the snapshot week level prevents merchant-level leakage within folds, but the design does not address how the sequence model handles merchants who were active at snapshot date but whose historical event stream predates the training window. If a merchant's full 90-day event history predates the training split cutoff, their pre-cutoff events function as training data for the sequence model but not for the tabular model (which only uses aggregates computed at snapshot time). This is an asymmetric information advantage for the sequence model that is not acknowledged.

Second, the churn label definition — '30 consecutive days of post-snapshot inactivity among merchants that had recent pre-snapshot processing activity' — requires knowing the 30 days following the snapshot date to assign a label. The design states all features are 'truncated at the snapshot timestamp,' which is correct for features. But the label itself is computed from future data. The concern is not leakage per se (this is standard in survival analysis), but the design does not address right-censoring: merchants near the test set's right edge whose 30-day inactivity window extends beyond the data collection endpoint will have missing or ambiguous labels. If these are simply excluded, the test set is not representative of the most recent operating environment.

Third, the design calls for retraining on train+validation before final test evaluation. For a sequence model with temporal embeddings, this retraining moves the effective training window forward in time, potentially altering the distribution of sequence lengths, event frequencies, and label prevalences in ways that affect the test-set predictions. The design does not discuss whether model selection hyperparameters (e.g., sequence length, embedding dimensions) were validated under the combined train+validation regime or only under the original train regime.

Fourth, the subgroup analysis by 'seasonal period' is mentioned but the experiment runs on 36 months of data, which means only two or three full seasonal cycles are available. Seasonal subgroup performance estimates will be based on a handful of cycles per season, giving wide confidence intervals that the design does not acknowledge.

Fifth, the experiment does not address the deployment gap: the model is evaluated as a ranker for an outreach queue, but the actual business decision (who receives outreach) will change future churn rates through intervention effects. If the best-performing churners receive retention outreach, the realized churn labels in any follow-on data will no longer reflect the counterfactual world the model was trained on. This is not a flaw in the experiment itself but is a critical missing caveat for interpreting whether PR-AUC on historical data translates to business impact.
all_issues_raised: Pre-cutoff event histories for the sequence model create an asymmetric information advantage: events that predate the training window boundary contribute to sequence context for the RNN but have no equivalent contribution to the tabular model's aggregates, making the comparison partly a data-access comparison rather than an architecture comparison.

Right-censoring at the test set's right edge: merchants whose 30-day post-snapshot inactivity window extends beyond the data collection endpoint will have ambiguous or excluded labels, making the test set unrepresentative of the most recent operating environment without an acknowledged censoring correction.

Retraining on train+validation before test evaluation shifts the effective training data distribution forward in time; hyperparameters validated on the original train split may not be optimal under the expanded regime, and this is not addressed.

Seasonal subgroup performance is estimated over only 2-3 full seasonal cycles in 36 months of data, yielding high-variance subgroup estimates whose uncertainty the design does not quantify or acknowledge.

The experiment evaluates a ranker for an outreach queue but does not acknowledge intervention feedback: historical churn labels reflect a no-outreach counterfactual, and PR-AUC on this data does not directly translate to business impact under a world where the model's recommendations drive retention actions.
all_issues_adjudicated: Pre-cutoff event histories for the sequence model create an asymmetric information advantage: events that predate the training window boundary contribute to sequence context for the RNN but have no equivalent contribution to the tabular model's aggregates, making the comparison partly a data-access comparison rather than an architecture comparison.

Right-censoring at the test set's right edge: merchants whose 30-day post-snapshot inactivity window extends beyond the data collection endpoint will have ambiguous or excluded labels, making the test set unrepresentative of the most recent operating environment without an acknowledged censoring correction.

Retraining on train+validation before test evaluation shifts the effective training data distribution forward in time; hyperparameters validated on the original train split may not be optimal under the expanded regime, and this is not addressed.

Seasonal subgroup performance is estimated over only 2-3 full seasonal cycles in 36 months of data, yielding high-variance subgroup estimates whose uncertainty the design does not quantify or acknowledge.

The experiment evaluates a ranker for an outreach queue but does not acknowledge intervention feedback: historical churn labels reflect a no-outreach counterfactual, and PR-AUC on this data does not directly translate to business impact under a world where the model's recommendations drive retention actions.
